text:
nstance,
if we are called upon to account for the origin of any
thing, we cannot do it, and all speculations are futile.
It is here that the theorist thinks himself strong; he
has a theory, plausible or not, and he calls upon you to
show him a better one, or to adopt his. Now, we may
not have fancy enough, in invention, to show him a
better one; but it does not follow that, because we
cannot, his must be correct. Where there is know-
ledge, there are no theories; and however amusing it
may be as pastime to make or receive theories, we
should not be positive of any value in them.
I have heard those who read the Bible according to
the letter, and not according to the spirit, call for a
better account of creation than that which is found in
the book of Genesis. Geology itself cannot give you
a better, because it cannot give you any; but it does
not follow, that a description of the existence of light
before the fountain of light is correct; and it is rather
too much to ask us to give credence to the manufacture
of a planet, or a system of planets, and to the placing
of them in the firmament in a few days; for geology
teaches us this, that the formation of a planet is the work
of millions of years. The surface may be explored and
examined far enough to convince us that it has not been
the work of a day; but that the different strata, or each
stratum, has been the temporal gathering of scores of
human generations, and was in action millions of years
before human beings were in act

and allegories about God,
the stuff of which religion is made, reaches none of
our understandings, and acts but as a fever on highly
susceptible and disordered nerves. It is priestcraft,
and not meant for our welfare. It is ignorance, and
can ouly cheat us. It is bombast, and can only con-
fuse us by making a mockery of the simplicity of real
knowledge. Let us be rid of it; let priestcraft cease;
and let there be built the foundation of useful know-
ledge and progressive improvement. The earth is
the sum total of man's knowledge: all the rest is to
him but a panoramic picture. It is on the earth only
that he can make experiments; it is here only that he
can analyse, watch the progress of creation, and cal-
culate consequences that affect him. If he measure
the distance of planet from plavet, ascertain the precise
motions of each planet, calculate eclipses or the return
of comets, what is he the wiser? He who can culti-
vate the earth, spin a thread, or throw a shuttle, is
wiser than Newton, and a more useful member of
society.
The ancient astronomical priests were called theists,
which signified runners, because theism is a treatise on
the course of the stars, and indicates or leaves the fair
inference that their minds were running after some-
thing that was of no use to them. We may put into
this class all the theists or religious people of the pre-
sent day and though the word atheist gives so much
offence, a word which I do not adopt because of the
perversion of its meanin

I begin the enquiry with the position, for argument,
that the human body is but one specimen or identity
of animal life, and that it has no property, in relation
to the words mind, soul, and spirit, but such as is com-
mon, more or less, to every other animal. The human
being, like every other animal, consists of but one prin-
ciple. In its formation, it is animal life. In its disso-
lution, it is animal death. While living, it is constantly
in a state of formation. When dead, its dissolution
instantly commences, and is the work of more or less
time, according to the state of the substance and the
circumstances by which it is surrounded. That which
is called mind, soul, or spirit, is nothing more than the
action and formation of the animal body while in a
state of life. There is neither mind, soul, nor spirit,
the moment death takes place and dissolution com-
mences; nor were there any such properties in the
state of life, to be separated and to exist distinctly
from the animal substance.

Call mind by whatever name or definition you please,
it is all resolved into bodily action. Say it is percep-
tion: what perceives? The body, the life? The per-
ception is an act in the formation and makes a part of
the system of life. With Locke, reduce the principle
of mind to sensation: what sensats, what feels? The
body. Say it is will: what wills? The body,
being, the life. Say it is thought, reason, judgment,
decision: what thinks, what reasons, what judges,
what decides, but the living body? It is as well to say
that walking is not performed by the motion of the
body, as that any act or faculty called mind is not per-
formed also by the motion of the body. There are dif-
ferent kinds of motions suited to different purpose

death is only that life is continued formation, or crea-

It has been said of matter, that it has extension, figure,
and other properties. This may be correctly said of
animal, vegetable, or mineral properties; but it cannot
be said of matter in general. Wood, metal, stone, are
substances which have, in whatever shape they are
seen, figure and extension and relative degrees of hard-
ness, heaviness, and durability; but this relates to the
identical character of wood, metal, or stone. Call them
matter, and you have no general principle on which to
rest; for a degree of fire that will consume the wood,
will only purify the metal, and make little or no im-
pression on stone; while other degrees of fire will gaseate

We have, lastly, to consider the word nature. This
word, while it is particularly used to express the secret
property or action of any identical thing, is also used
to cover our ignorance of the general whole. It is a
similar word to matter, spirit, soul, god; but an idle

and we only use two words, or three words, where one

steppes of Siberia, even to the frontiers of China

teresting branch of zoography a principal object of
investigation.

In the thirty-eighth verse, we read: "But if any
man be ignorant, let him be ignorant.' We say: If
any man be ignorant, let him get knowledge. I do
not profess a knowledge of the Greek language; but J
should be much surprised, if the original Greek would
not bear a better translation of many parts of this
chapter. You see, sirs, I am a very bad expounder
of the sacred Scripture; for, having no faith in the
metamorphosis of meanings, I find fault where there
should be no fault.

view of a delusion and an imposture.

cerity of feeling, or rather of expression according to

Then why should we wonder at the vagaries of the
brain? If the fear of present and immediate punish-

The noises made under religious excitement are like
the involuntary sounds uttered under any other kind of
excitement; but when an attempt is made to put an
interpretation or a meaning to those sounds, they be-
come a rank imposture. When they are described as
unearthly or superhuman sounds, they are tricks.
Excitement, even to hysterics, is seen in all religious
assemblies where fervour passes as religious virtue. In
the fields of America, whole congregations may be
seen rolling about in hysterics, under the excitement of
two or three days' religious exercise. In the assem.
blies and love feasts of those who are called Ranters
in England similar exhibitions are made. The moving

duce the vagaries of the unknown tongues, hysterics,
exclamations, gesticulations, and all the motions which
the spirit can make.

Hail, gentle Ists! welcome to our sight
Child of Osiris! star of mental light!
Since Father, Son, and Holy Ghost have fa l'd,
And Hell o'er Heaven has hitherto prevailed,
Be thou our oracle, be thine the task,
To rend the veil and tear away the mask
From crafty priests; that superstition's slaves
May see the chains imposed by pious knaves!
Pursue his track who learnt to read the skies❤
And thou shal find that mental paradise,
Where no beguiling serpents cause dispute,
And where no trees bring forth forbidden fruit
Then whether Isis!, Sybil, or an Eve,
Take feely of the fruit, and freely give,
With bold es ays inspire the rising youth,
And let thy lips proclaim the sacred truth.
Instruct from nature's book, whence wisdom flow,
And show that knowledge can no farther go;
Should even 'ancy strive to stretch beyond,
And make enquiries, Nature would respond,
Rash man! is written in my fix'd decree
That thou shalt have no other god but me!
When superstition from the mind is driven,
And the true god, the glorious light of heaven,
Whose throne's usurped by a fictitious lord,

The suffering of Prometheus was an enchainment
to a rock, in a state of crucifixion, or extended limbs,
and as an emblem of the vitality and never-dying
principle of reason; a vulture daily feeds on his liver,
as tyranny daily feeds or preys on growing reason.
The suffering of Christ, which is a more common
picture to our view, was a crucifixion on a post with
a transverse beam, the body sustained by rusty and
ruthless nails, and with a fifth wound in the side, made
by the spear of tyranny.
Both Prometheus and Christ are represented as up-
braiding the cruelty of their condition, and the tyranny
of the supreme or ruling power; and, after a death and
sinking into hell, both rise again and ascend to
heaven.

Well, having reduced man to a mere automaton;

The clock goes tick, tick, and divides time into hours

It has no power of invention, no choice of direction;

is furnished with such organs, faculties, and capabili-

him the stamp of rationality; I mean his reason, that
noble prerogative of human nature; it is this glorious
faculty that raises him not only above the mechanical
machine, but above every other known animal in

and a compound of phy-
sical and mental faculties, but he appears to me to be
also a compound of necessity and choice.
For example, if an object is presented to me for
my acceptance; an apple, for instance, I am cer-
tainly necessitated to either take it or leave it. On
this proposition no division can arise, because all the
world agree to the fact. But then comes the question
of choice of action; or as some term it freewill: that
is, whether I have a discretionary power to take or
leave the apple. In the latter case you will per
ceive, and I trust acknowledge, that the evidence of
necessity is not by any means so conclusive as in the
former. And this compound of action and re-action,
compulsion and repulsion, of necessity and choice,
is observable in all the operations of the physical
senses, when acted upon by external objects. Ex-
ternal objects act upon the senses with an arbitrary,
while the senses re-act upon external objects with a
discretionary power.
I have now stated my view of this very important
question, and shall conclude by observing, that your
kingdom may be of this world; but if it is not to be
established, until a sufficient number of persons to fill
it are converted to your faith and principles, it will
certainly not be established in this age, nor in the
next, unless you are content with a very small king-
dom. I remain, &c. ALLEN DAVENPORŤ,

animal and vegetable life. There is one general princi-

which we call spirit or mind. We mark the distinc

This human creature is a wonderfully varied article
in the scale of nature, and is wholly unfitted to be the
general subject of a system, which is one of the ills of
necessity, becomes a tyranny, and fetters the spirit of
liberty. The varied character of man requires for its
welfare the most unlimited liberty. Competition and
emulation are its spirit and passion, and any attempt
to subdue them, will, I fear, lead to no good result, if
it were practised with power to be tyrannical. Man
is nowhere more dignified than as the moral master of
his own family, surrounded by a kind wife, affectionate
children, and assistants, who love and respect him for
his care of their welfare. Any attempt to reduce this
character, and to sink it into the soldier-like character
of submission to a system, that is not, perhaps the best,
and to a discipline irksome to individuals, is to seek
to subdue the moral and the physical energies of man.
Every animal is a world in itself, and man not less so.
The family man is like a solar system, the sun and its
planets. He may move in the space required, with-
out clashing with any other family man; but to make
a uniformity of motion in each family, appears to me,
as impracticable, as to make a uniformity of motion in
the varied solar systems.

This notice or treatise, will be divided into two dis-

if the return of the now approaching comet had not
called them forth into importance.

etymologically considered, means a star with a head
of hair. The most luminous point, seen at or near the
centre, is called the nucleus. The nebulosity, or foggy
luminous aureola, which surrounds the nucleus on every

real stars. The simultaneous observations made on

Since the time of Tycho, who made this discovery,
it has been ascertained that comets revolve round the
sun, according to regular laws, similar to those which
regulate the planetary movements, only that their orbits
are very long ellipses, of which the sun always occu

Definition of terms is the essentiality of philosophi-
cal discussion, and the beginning of this subject will
be to ask-
WHAT IS EVIL?
The words, good and evil, as opposite principles,
can have no relation beyond animal life. Sensation is
all that can be subject to them. They are the moral,
to animal life, of the more expressive words, liberty
and necessity, of which I treated in my last discourse,
so as to leave nothing, in my judgment, unsaid.
Good, to the individual, is that which is pleasurable.
Good, to society, is that which produces the greatest
happiness of the greatest number.
Evil, consequently, is that which is painful to the
individual, or to the majority of a community.
Good and evil are also relative terms. A state of
things that would be good to one individual, may be
an evil to another. For instance, an annuity of a
hundred pounds a year would be a good thing to a
poor man; but a reduction of income, from two hun-
dred to one hundred a year, becomes an evil. So that
there is no standard of good and evil but in animal
sensation. That is a variable standard, affected and
regulated by circumstances.
As far as man is concerned, we trace evil to his
sensations. Society is made up of human sensations,
and we may reach the origin of evil, by an inquiry
into what is painful to those sensations, or in what
cases evil preponderates over good.

RSE
OF
THE EDITRESS,
Delivered in her Lecture-Room, 1, Bouverie-street,
Fleet-street, on Sunday, the 15th of April, and sub-
sequent days of that week.
ON THE PURSUIT OF KNOWLEDGE.
MANKIND, like every other animal, is born without
knowledge, to the entire absence of an idea, and with-
out even the rudiments of mind. The education of
each infant begins at the point of experience at which
its parents or its nurse has arrived. The greater that
experience, the greater the facility presented to the
tuition of the infant.
The acquisition of knowledge is only limited by the
time and power of man to acquire it. The pursuit is
therefore interminable; and it is one of the pleasures
of life that it is so. It encourages the infant, gives
useful employment to manhood, and softens the aspe-
rities of age.
Addition is the principle of growth, both in body
and mind; and careful daily addition is the way by
which to make the best pursuit of knowledge. The
human body may be made an interminable store-house
of knowledge; but the more may be laid up, as atten-
tion is paid to the constant addition, which, from day
to day, may be acquired. A day, in which no new
knowledge is gained, is a day lost. Titus exclaimed,
I have lost a day, when he recollected that he had
spent one in carousal, and had done no one good deed;
so we all may say we have lost a day, when we have
passed it without storing new knowledge.
But even the word knowledge requires a definition.
It is not knowledge to have committed a

proper and necessary mechanism of surgery, is fast
rising above the empiricism of the physician, while the

Knowledge may be defined to be the possession of
ideas applicable to the uses of life, by which the com-
forts of life may be increased; and this is that which
distinguishes one man from another, and man in general
from other animals.
There are ideas which are not applicable to the uses
of life, or false ideas. Such are all the ideas which
make up superstition.
There are others, again, which are no further useful,
than as they tend to overthrow superstition. Such
are those which are associated with the learning ne-
cessary to unravel the mysteries and allegories of the
ancients.
There are ideas of the kind necessary to overthrow
superstition that are otherwise useful, inasmuch as they
allay the fears of life, and correct the conceptions of
the human brain. Such are all deep investigations of
science in astronomy, in chemistry, in geológy, and
in all the relative sciences connected with natural
history.

of knowledge to any degree, creates new appetite as it
proceeds, and never palls. It should be the ingredient
mixed up with all other pleasures, to prevent a surfeit.

after truth, we must equally discard presumption and

"When we have taken this view of things, observe
how all abstruse questions disappear; how all science
is simplified; all knowledge rendered easy and fami

whatever science we apply, that is, to whatever part of
matter, or to whichever of its qualities, we direct our
attention, we shall, in all probability, make important,
because true, discoveries. It is the philosophy of
nature in general, or any of those subdivisions of it,

astronomy, geometry, &c.; the moment we occupy

Our su-
perstitions decrease as our attainments multiply; and
the fervour of our religion declines; as we draw nearer

clusion, based upon accumulated facts, is, as we have
seen, that matter alone is at once the thing acting, and
the thing acted upon; eternal in duration, infinitely
various and varying in appearance; never diminishing
in quantity, and always changing in form. Without
some knowledge of what is styled natural philosophy,
or physics, no individual can attain this conclusion.
And in a certain stage of that knowledge, more or less
advanced, according to the acuteness of the intellect,
it will be impossible for any individual, not mentally
obtuse, to shun that conclusion. This truth is one of in-
finite importance. The moment we consider the hostility
directed against what is called atheism, as the natural
result of deficient information, the mind must be diseased
which could resent that hostility. And perhaps a simple
statement of the truth would best lead to examination
of the subject, and to the conversion of mankind.

M. ARAGO ON COMETS.
•
(Continued from The Times of March 16.)
Here the Moon will serve as a point of comparison.
The moon causes the tides of the ocean: mathematically speak-
ing, the comet of 1811 ought to have produced analagous tides,
but, as no one observed any such tides, we must admit that they
did not amount to an appreciable quantity. But the height of a
tide is proportioned to the attractive power. We know that the
lunar tides are very great, and that the cometary tides (if any) are
insensible; hence, it is clear that the cometary action on the
earth bears no proportion to that of the moon; and as to any
calorific effects of the great comet of 1811, the most delicate in-
struments have not shown their existence. Can, then, the wine-
growers expect any results from the comet of 1832 ?

been adopted too generally. It is true that for the most part the
tail is placed behind the comet, so as to stream out in a direction
opposed to the sun; but the line which joins those two luminaries
hardly ever corresponds with the axis of the til. Sometimes the
want of coincidence of these two lines is very great; indeed, in
some cases, the tail stands out at right angles from the line of
conjunction. In fact, it has been ascertained that the tail has
constantly an inclination towards the region which the comet has
left, as if, in its motion through a gaseous medium, the matter of
which the tail was formed had been more powerfully acted upon,
or resisted, than was experienced by the nucleus.
The tails become much larger, that is, wider, as they lengthen
out from the comet. In the middle of them is seen a dark band,
which divides them into two distinct, and often equal parts. An-
cient observers considered this band as the shadow of the body of
the comet. This explanation cannot be applied to the tails which
point towards the sun; perhaps we shall suit our hypothesis to

a far-fetched etymological system, but the application

in saying this, it does not follow that God is a person,

to a person, but both to a principle. Then the ques-
tion catechetically and properly arises, as to what prin-
ciple. The answer is easy: the highest principle known
to man, or the foundation of all known principles.

universe, or the known planetary system. Morally,
it relates to human knowledge, and is a supposed
fountain of perfect knowledge. But this supposition
is an error; for there is no source or fountain of
knowledge beyond the human race. Man, in the

The nebulosity of comets, when attentively considered, presents
also inextricable difficulties. Without doubt it seems easy and
natural, at the first glance, to suppose comets to be simply masses
of permanent gas, and vapours thrown out from the nucleus, by
the constant influence of the solar rays; but what becomes, under
this system, of the luminous concentric envelopes seen round
some comets? and why should the nucleus be eccentric, and
generally so towards the sun, though sometimes so on the oppo-
site side?
The different magnitudes of the nebulosities of comets are
worthy of deep attention, and Hevelius, rising above all system,
announced at once that the diameters of the nebulosities increased
in proportion as comets receded from the sun! Pingré had also
perceived this, but only dared announce it in an incidental man-
ner Although I do not mean to justify Pingré's hesitation, yet
in his day, considering, too, all the difficulties of admeasurement,
&c., we cannot wonder at it; and, indeed, it was hard to believe
that a gaseous mass should, in proportion as it receded from the
sun, that is, as it plunged into colder regions, be considerably
expanded, instead of being condensed. But, thanks to the comet
of "the short period," the observation of Hevelius is now es-
tablished beyond all doubt, and is admitted amongst fully recog-
nized scientific truths. I now give a table of the variations which

From what I have said of the general tenuity of come's, those

The comet of 1770 is the one which has hitherto passed near-
est to us, according to known observations. Its smallest distance
from the earth was 602,000 leagues-that is, six times as far as
the moon.
La Place has shown that the action of the earth on
this comet augmented the period of its revolution by two days,
and the re-action of the comet ought in like manner to lengthen
the time of the earth's revolution round the sun; but observations
have proved, that in 1770 the length of our year was not in-
creased by one second. lu fact, this comet twice traversed the
space in which the satellites of Jupiter move, without causing
amongst them the slightest alteration.

reason to suppose that comets have ever fallen into the sun, or
into any of the fixed stars?
The comet of 1680, at its perihelion, was only one-sixth of the
diameter of the sun from its surface. In a region so near this
immense globe, the atmosphere which surrounds it may have a
considerable density, and may produce on bodies passing through
it effects which must not be neglected. This would be true, in
particular, in regard to comets whose rapidity of motion at the
perihelion is considerable, and whose density is inconsiderable.
The necessary effect of such an atmosphere on the comet of
1680, would be to diminish its tangential or centrifugal velocity:
but if any celestial body is retarded in its movement, and thereby
loses part of its centrifugal force, the centrifugal counterbalancing
force at once becomes the preponderating force, and the revolv-
ing body quits its curve to fall towards the centre of attraction.
Thus, then, the comet of 1680 would pass nearer to the sun in
that year than it did at its former revolution; and as this ap-
proximation will be continued at each return to the perihelion,
the comet of 1680 will end by falling into the sun.
These reasonings rest on demonstrable mechanical principles;
we must only admit that in our present ignorance of the density
and arrangement of the solar atmosphere, as well as of the orbit
and nature of the comet of 1680, it is impossible to calculate in
how many ages the catastrophe I have alluded to will occur.

law must have been impressed on the motions of these bodies at

Buffon is the first who ventured to mount to the origin of the
planets and their satellites, and to account for this similarity of
motion. He supposed that a comet falling obliquely on the sun,
grazed or furrowed the surface of it, and drove off a quantity of
fluid matter, the lighter parts of which, driven to the greatest
distance, concentrated and formed the planets Saturn and Jupiter,
whose density is, in fact, not great, while the heavier portion,
driven to a less distance, produced Mercury, Venus, the Earth,
and Mars, and that thus the planets were in their origin so heated
as to be in a state of fusion, and that they then all took regular
forms, and, in cooling, assumed the appearances we now see.
Some of the objections which have been urged against Buffon's
system might be answered particularly those which object to the
greatness of the mass to be driven off from the sun by the sup-
po ed shock of a comet, for the whole of the planets and their
satellites do not amount to one eight-thousandth part of the mass
of the sun.

ON THE IMPROVEMENT OF THE MIND.
THE cultivation of the mind is the most noble sub-
ject that can occupy the time, and engage the attention
of mankind; it is agreeable and remunerating; for it
yields both amusement and profit. The advantages
resulting from it can be observed every day of our
lives; they can be recognized in our ordinary transac-
tions, as well as in those of superior importance. It is
an ennobling employment; for it promotes the comfort
and dignifies the character of man. It crowns him with
laurels less fading than those gained at the olympic
games, which were in most cases but the trophies of
physical excellence, rewards which frequently exercise
an injurious influence upon the intellectual faculties,
making us more easily the victims of those unhallowed
propensities and passions that are hostile to our best
interests. To enlighten the understanding, we must
have an obstinate perseverance, an insatiable desire,
an ardour sufficiently lively to surmount all difficulties
in acquiring it. If we are sensible that our personal
perseverance, our desire and ardour are incompatible
to the task, we must take courage by the example,
the experience, and the entreaties of our friends, pic-
turing occasionally to excite ambition, the applause
and rewards which are due to learning as its legitimate
inheritance. Some are so eminently gifted with na-
tural qualifications for study, that what is often a toil

In man, life is all that has been described; to which
we may add, that of which most animals are suscep-
tible, the recipient mental faculty, the faculty of edu-
cation; the speculative faculty, or that of hope, and
prospect, and calculation; an improved interchange of
through the means of speech. But, with these
advantages, man has his disadvantages. The sensibi-
lities which lead to increased pleasure, are also sus-
ceptible of increased pain. He is as often the man
of sorrows as of pleasures; and human wisdom should
be employed to lessen the former, and to increase the
latter.
Life has also its degrees of measure or strength. It
begins imperceptibly, and grows to robustness, te-
nacity, and long endurance; but, to an individual, it
is never eternal. The very principle of its existence
forbids the idea of individual eternity, because it is
founded upon the principles of wear and tear; and
the structure of the body is but the garment of life.
The conceit, that spirit can retain an identity with-
out the aid of the body, is that of superstition and
madness. The idea is not founded upon any principle
whatever, for spirit is the quality of the body; cannot
be but for the creation of the body; and is as much
the emanation of the body, as heat or cold, or any
other quality or function. As well may we expect
the music without the instrument and the performer,
as the spirit to be without the body. Resurrection of
the body is also physically impossible; for, in life,

Knowledge gives to man the advantage of recording
his life, and thus a remembrance of it in others is pro-
longed; but this adds nothing to the length of indi-
vidual life. The beauty and fragrance of a rose pre-
serves to us a remembrance of the flower; but the
flower itself survives not its month, without some arti-
ficial aid. The best of men is but the flower of a few
years; but lo, he droopeth and dieth, his beauty pass-
eth away; and wonderful must he have been, to sur-
vive the memory of a century.

in everlasting fire can nowhere exist, and has nowhere

Volcanoes produce earthquakes. And wherever there
is fire, there is change in the material that feeds it.
Even brimstone consumes, slow and noxious as it is
while consuming; and brimstone itself, if the whole
earth were changed to brimstone, would not furnish
everlasting fire. Had the sun ever been a body of
fire, it would not have been fuel for a year's consump-
tion. Those chimnies of the bowels of the earth, which
we call volcanocs, have only periodical eruptions, at
greatly distant periods; and even in those we may look
in vain for everlasting fire.
The everlasting fire of the Sacred Scriptures is the
spirit that should not be quenched, in which mankind
should be baptized; the spirit of knowledge. But
hell, as vulgarly preached, is the borrowed Tartarus
of the Pagans, and is emblematical of a state of igno-
rance, as Heaven, Paradise, the Garden of Eden, or
Elysium, is of a state of knowledge. As there is no-
thing to man beyond life and after death, so all these
poetic figures must reasonably relate to something that
passes through life.

we can only know a thing by its immediate contact
with our senses, so is all knowledge compounded of
the accurately observed, accumulated, and agreeing
sensations of mankind.
Greatly, very greatly to be desired, is a just mode
of instruction. It would not only shorten the road of
knowledge, it would carpet it with flowers. We should
then tread it in childhood with smiles of cheerfulness;
and, as we followed its pleasant course, horizon after
horizon would open upon us, delighting and improv-
ing our minds and feelings through life, unto our lat-
est hour. But if it is of the first importance to be
launched aright in infancy, the moment we distinctly
perceive what knowledge is, we may, at any age, start
boldly for its attainment.

the nature and object of just knowledge, together with
the means proper for its attainment. We discovered
these means to be two: a judicious education, and a
free spirit of inquiry.
From the first and best means, a judicious education,
we, of the present generation, are unfortunately ex-
cluded. Wherever our lot may have been cast, or
whatever may be our attainments, we must all be con-
scious that we are what we are, in spite of many dis-
advantages; and that, however wise or good our
vanity may pronounce us to be, we should have been
much wiser, and, consequently, better and happier,
had a judicious education more carefully developed
our tender faculties, and brought order and accuracy
to all our nascent ideas. But the forest is grown; and,
straight or crooked, the trees have to stand pretty much
as early circumstances have inclined them. Still,
something may be done; nay! if we bring fearless
and determined spirits to the work, much may be done
much for ourselves, and everything for our descend-to
ants. It rests with us to command, for the rising
generation, that education, whose want we, in our own
case, deplore. It rests with us to open, with a golden
key, the gates of just knowledge for our children;
and to marshal them in those smooth, broad, pleasant
paths, which we ourselves have never trod. Equally
true it is, that we cannot, for ourselves, command that
first, best means, for obtaining the first, best good.

Deity in the book of Job. We can comprehend ef-
fects, which are local and contingent; but we cannot
trace causes, which extend themselves indefinitely and
escape our means of observation. We may compre-

it is planted on the brain. That perception is an ac-
cumulating principle, we know, by experience; but,
what it is in principle and process of action, we know
not. We know that it varies in degree and character,
with the person or the thing perceiving and perceived,
exhibiting moral effects in the animal world and phy-
sical effects in the vegetable and the mineral. It is a
principle of change; for a new perception is a change
made in the brain or body generally. It is the first
principle and distinction of animal life, and is also the
principle of change in every other thing,
God is knowledge, and the more knowledge we get
the more we know God. Knowledge is a compound
property made up of sensations, which are perceptions:
so we may say God is a perception; and that defini-
tion applies both morally and physically to the author
of all things.

A Discourse, by FRANCES WRIGHT, delivered in the Hall of
Science, New York, and in other parts of the United States of
America.
IN our preceding discourses we have investigated,
first, the nature and object of just knowledge; se-
condly, the means for attaining that knowledge. It
remains for us to distinguish those parts or divisions
of knowledge, with which it most concerns us to be
familiar.
First, acquaintance with ourselves. We must allow
this to be important. If anything concerns us, it
should be our own bodies and minds. What do we
understand of their structure? what of their faculties
and powers? If we understand not these, how may
we preserve the health of either? How may we
avoid injurious habits, understand our sensations, pro-
fit by experience, and establish ourselves in bodily.
temperance and mental sobriety?

Before we can proceed to examine our opinions, we
must ascertain facts drawn from the attentive obser-
vation of matter. We must know the anatomy of the
matter composing our own bodies, and that of the
matter composing all other bodies. We must familiar-
ize our senses and our understandings with the multi-to
form and yet unvarying phenomena of nature. We
must know what does happen, and what does not hap-
pen. We must trace in the physical world, cause to
cause; or, more properly, occurrence to occurrence;
and whenever we do not perceive the clenching link
between two occurrences, we must not imagine it; we
must say we do not know it, and we must go, with our
five senses open, in search of it. Had human beings,
in all ages of the world, done this, where should we
not now be in just knowledge? It is time that we
seek out the right road. We have groped long enough
in error; lived long enough in fairy-land; dreamed
more than enough of things unseen, and causes un-
known. We have, indeed, dreamed so much and ob-
served so little, that our imaginations have grown
larger than the world we live in, and our judgments.
have dwindled down to a point.

Man originally existed, and, now, to a great extent,
doth exist, on a par with all other animals. He has
not a natural faculty, but in which he is excelled by
some other animal. It is in his moral faculties, in
his little knowledge, that he becomes their superior,

as an animal, must have come into the world as all
other animals came, struggling on through a helpless
condition, and gaining strength through an experience
of the means necessary to a provision for wants,
for a time the prey of stronger animals, and gaining
his dominion over them as he gained knowledge.
The aborigines of New South Wales were found to
have made but little progress in knowledge; though,
by education, they are as susceptible of knowledge

past all means of being traced; but the creation of
mind may be morally calculated, in its progression;

cred Scriptures, and the revelation of the sacred
mysteries.

THE POOR STUDENT'S PASSION.
WHAT pen in prose can tell the care,
What muse in rhyming lines declare,
In what poetic strains indulge,
The latent ardour to divulge,
Which men of honest spirits feel,
Their humble duties to fulfil,
To Youth-whose manners and whose mind
Are to be cultur'd and refin'd:
Who much resemble, when well taught,
The ship, with pearls so costly, fraught;
And who, through life, a conduct steers,
Unknown to vulgar eyes and ears.
Yea, eagle-like, aloft will soar,
And countless orbs and worlds explore;
And, led by astronomic light,
With rapt'rous pleasure and delight,
Will range the road, by learning trod,
To the perennial throne of GOD!
And taste, and drink, with unmix'd joy,
The draughts of bliss which cannot cloy.
O what amazing scenes, behold,
The worlds of science do unfold!-
What glories circle those around,
With what delights their days abound,
Who labour, study, and desire
Such erudition to acquire ;-
This wealth is such as long will stay,
When shining dust will fly away,
And still remain a valued store,
To make them rich, but never poor.
Let friends forsake, let foes combine,
If wealth like this may but be mine;
Let sisters scorn, let brothers hate,
Mine I esteem a happy state;
Let fathers, with fierce anger, frown,
And their own flesh and blood disown;
Let those who bare us cease to love,

NOTICE. The Glossary for the Bible, inserted
last week, is improved and reprinted in a small pam-
phlet for twopence. It will be of vast importance, to
the construction of future minds, to put this little
tract into the hands of children before they begin to

dividuals to circulate them gratuitously, or as pre-
sents, they will be sold at 8s. per hundred, and
eighteen pence per dozen.
THE MAMMOTH.-Among the natural curiosities
preserved in the Museum at St. Petersburgh, is the
skeleton of a mammoth that was found buried in an
iceberg in Siberia. As the ice gradually dissolved in
a summer more than usually warm, bears attacked
the flesh, which was in a state of preservation, and
destroyed the skin, but the skeleton was secured with
the exception of a single foot. It now stands close
to that of a large elephant, and though the one is
not much less in height than the other, a comparison
of their joints shows how superior the mammoth
must have been in strength and bulk. From a part of
the skin which has been preserved, it appears that
the animal was furnished with long hair, a fact afford-
ing strong presumptive evidence that it inhabited the
cold latitude in which the body was discovered, and

This theory on the common origin of these four planets was as-
sented to generally; but, disagreement arose amongst philosophers,
when it became necessary to assign a cause for the splitting of the
great planet. Some, having recourse to internal gases and commio-

this doctrine of the internal explosion, and declare that a heavenly
body can be broken only by the striking of a comet.

will, however, give some singular reasons on which the advocates for

MIGRATION OF BIRDS.-Whatever theory of in-
stinct may be finally fixed upon as the most correct
and philosophical, (to account for the migratory
movements of birds,) it is obvious that we cut rather
than unite the gordian knot when we talk of the fore-
sight of the brute creation. We might as well talk of
the foresight of a barometer. There can be little
doubt that birds, prior to their migratory movements,
are influenced by atmospherical changes, or other
physical causes, which, however beyond the sphere
of our perceptions, are sufficient for their guidance.
That they are not possessed of the power of divination
may be exemplified by the following instance:-The
winter of 1822 was so remarkably mild throughout
Europe, that primroses came generally into flower by
the end of December,-rye was in ear by the middle
of March, and vines, in sheltered situations, blossomed
about the end of that month,-so that an assured
and unchecked spring was established at least four
or five weeks earlier than usual;-yet neither the
cuckoo nor the swallow arrived a single day before
their accustomed periods. They are indeed, beauti-
fully and wisely directed.-" Yea, the stork in the

he body that dies.
So, to speak of "the immortality of the soul," other
than as it may distinguish itself by deeds while alive,
is to talk nonsense, to use words without meaning; is
to talk about immortal death.
Mr. Paine has observed, that "the belief in a fu-
ture state is a rational belief, founded upon facts vi-
sible in the creation; for it is not more difficult to
believe that we shall exist hereafter, in a better state
and form than at present, than that a worm should be-
come a butterfly, and quit the dunghill for the atmos
phere, if we did not know it as a fact."
Mr. Paine's supposed analogy is not good. A worm
becomes a butterfly, as an infant boy becomes a man,
not in a change from death to life, but in the growth of
life; the belief in a future state to existing individuals
is not, therefore, a rational belief. Mr. Paine was a
very superstitious man, and wrote on theology to pre-
-serve a superstition.
It is only necessary to ask ourselves the proper
meaning of words, and to look at the facts of nature
to assure ourselves that the words "immortality of the
-soul" are words without meaning, and, as far as a
meaning can be inferred, it is false in physics and in
morals.

The celebrated philosopher, Alex. De Humboldt,
speaking of the Georama, which was proposed once
to be erected in London, said,-" In a country where
so great a part of the population apply themselves to
the study of geography, this invention, which appeals
so energetically to the senses, cannot fail to meet with
that success of which the Georama is deserving in
every respect, &c."
In a memorial presented to the French Minister of
public instruction, this celebrated geographer sub-
joined the following additional testimonial:-
"It would afford me pleasure if my recommenda-
tion were of the least service or advantage to the
grand and useful establishment of the Georama, which
I have never visited without reaping some benefit by
it, or without improving my knowledge of geography."

What, then, I am most anxious we should bear in
mind, is, that we have all to be learners. Ask the most
experienced philosopher, whose patient mind has ex-
plored all the paths of discovered knowledge, and added
new wealth to the stores of the human intellect-ask
him, and he will tell you he is yet a pupil. Ask him,
and he will tell you that the span of human life suf-
ficeth not to explore the whole even of the observable
wonders of nature-wondrous at least to our limited
perceptions and finite existence; while, beyond the
stretch of our vision, as evinced by the microscope
and telescope, he will tell you that the phenomena of
nature extend through the infinitely little and the in-
finitely great, in duration and extension, without limit
as without end. Oh, who hath said that science
teaches pride, when with her alone is humility!
Who hath said, that to study the field of nature can
generate self-conceit, when he who should know all
that by human senses and faculties can be known,
would only best understand that he knew, as it were,
nothing!
An ingenious poet hath sung:
"A little learning is a dangerous thing;"

knowledge. Drink of the right spring, and, drink

wise. Yet this, above all things: speculate not far-

Oh! let us not fear, that within the atmosphere of
our own world, in the powers and wants of our own
nature, and in the woes of human life, as originating
in human error, that we may not find a field of inquiry
more than sufficient to fill our time, enchain our
thoughts, and call into action every latent faculty and
feeling of our nature.

sume, among a people boasting themselves free, (and
free, rightly interpreted, would mean rational,) the
place of religion. Let us, instead of speculating and
disputing where we can discover nothing, observe and
inquire where we can discover everything.

one animal to another, and of one class of things
to another class of things; teaching superstition
to be a necessary first principle, and habituating

it can never be hoped that the man should be again
divested.
Mr. Pinnock has made great improvement on school
books; but he has a root of the original sin remaining.
His new publication, "The Guide to Knowledge,"
still embodies this error.
I have seen a new publication, entitled "The
Doctor," published every Wednesday, at a penny,
something like which would be very useful as a school
book; for I would make the science of medicine a part
of natural history to be taught to children in schools.
That which I propose is not difficult to be accom-
plished, as far as the construction of books is in ques-

free from errors, and were much trammelled by the
churchified state of the public mind, they taught us
the principle of analysis in physics and in morals, and
have laid a foundation for future moral culture that
cannot be departed from. Though Newton wandered,
he had a correct first principle, that nothing but mat-
ter could move matter, and thus asserted the mecha-
nical property of motion. Locke annihilated the idea
of duality of soul, spirit, life, and mind, by so ana-
lysing it, as to show it in the sensations of the animal
body.
Priestley reached the proper theory of the gases,
at the close of the last century, and, by explaining
their materialism, left no way in the world for the
further dwelling of personified spirits. The curiosity
of these men led them to cut deeper at the root of
superstition, than, perhaps, they had any desire to
do; deeper, at least, than they had moral courage
to avow.
The appearance of many twopenny periodical po-

them has broken out anew in penny periodicals, in
almost every branch of literature, of science, and
of politics, and they cling to their old laws in vain.

THE present is an era of unparalleled interest to the
moral observer, i. e. to him who considers all occur-
rences with a view to their influence on human society.
The principle of change is in all nature, but the
principle of improvement is only (so far as observa-
tion has enabled us to ascertain) in the nature of man.
The scientific eye traces the convulsions of our
earth's solid sphere back, through millions of untold
generations, to eras lost in time, when animals of other
form from those which now move on its surface, ranged
from pole to pole, and (apparently in the absence of
man, whose organic remains seem of more recent ori-
gin) fed on another vegetable kingdom, or preyed on
each other as we now sce their successors. Or let us
observe what is passing around us in the field of exist-
ing nature: Each season brings its vicissitudes, each
passing instant its changes-in the herb, in the flower,
in the forest, in the mountain, in the jewel of the secret
mine; in the vast bed of the ocean-dividing conti-
nents, engulfing or revealing islands, approaching or
receding from its wonted boundaries, until the land-
marks of other days are no more guides to the traveller
or the mariner of these; in all the forms of matter,
whether gaseous, fluid, or solid, whether animate, or,
to our perception, inanimate; in every particle and
unit atom that fills its place, and exercises its agency,
through the endless succession of existence and dura-

Your trinity or godhead, my dear Maria, is an
absolute negation of all sense, analogy, or comparison;
because you make a trinity of persons, instead of a
trinity of principles. There is no other correct idea
of analogy or comparison for three persons in one
person, than as mother and her unborn twin children;
but of a principle physical or moral, divisible into
three, there are many analogies and proofs. The ac-
quisition, possession, and communication of knowledge,
is a tripartite principle, a trinity in unity, and such is
the real meaning of the Christian religion in its rela-
tion to, and bearing upon moral or human nature.
Mineral, vegetable, and animal nature, may be con-
sidered a trinity in unity, in the physical world.
Earth, water, and the atmospheric air form a trinity
in unity for the production of all vegetable matter.
We have many analogies of this kind in the physical
and moral world; but not one that justifies the setting
up of a trinity of persons to represent the godhead.
Nor do I refer you to unitarian Christianity as any
thing better. That is a pretence to make religion
rational; but it is a conceit that falls short of its pur-
pose.

It is this curious contradictory feature of nature
which preserves the harmony and order of the uni-
verse. It is the never failing concord of these two
extremes, in themselves so perfect in opposition, which
prevents nature from presenting a scene of infinite di-
vision and discord; elements of one sex from warring
with each other, and the whole universe from going
into wreck. Thus, for instance, the motion of the
heavenly bodies, in their annual orbits round the sun,
is caused and maintained by the union, or what is
called the resolution of two forces. The sun draws
the planet in toward itself, by what is called the ac-
tion of gravitation. If this action were unresisted,
all the planets would be precipitated toward the sun,
and the dissolution of the system would immediately
take place. But to counteract this powerful ten-
dency, there is, as usual, another power of an oppo-
site nature which propels the planet in a contrary or
transverse direction. This power, if left to itself,
would carry the planet away into the wilderness of
space; but by the opposition of these two forces a
fair balance is created, and the planet, the object of
their peaceful contention, moves in great order and
regularity in its solar orbit. But observe the wisdom
of nature. These two forces do not act in direct or
perfect opposition to each other, if the one force drew

Let us examine other two extremes, namely, matter
and mind, and we shall come to the same conclusions.
Of mind we can say nothing at all. It would be
foolish in us to affirm that it existed independent of
matter, because this is an idea which is to us perfectly
incomprehensible. Mind is invisible, and, in all pro-
bability, is the effect of organization of matter. But
whatever be the primary cause of the existence of
mind, no man can dispute its being, and attributes of
sensation, memory, and judgment. But to this con-

without mind, is confusion and discord, and mind,

method, or plan or design for method, and design is
nothing else than mind; and if mind existed without

It is impossible to fix upon any number better cal-
culated to represent the godhead or nature, than the
number three. All nature is full of it. The first or
Father, represents the one extreme of nature: the third,
or Holy Ghost, represents the other extreme of nature,
and the second, or Son, the union of two extremes.
The second therefore, which unites the two extremes
together, is the proper representative of rest and hap
piness; for, out of this, there is nothing but discord and
division. This explains the reason, why the prece-

cord and evil, but the reign of the Son is said to be a

This bears the strongest resemblance to nature at large,
as we shall soon find, by examining the various Trini-
ties which the universe presents.
The first, and one of the most beautiful specimens
of the Trinity of nature to which I shall direct your
attention, is the Trinity in light. There are only
three primitive uncompounded colours in light;
namely, blue, red, and yellow. These three blended
together, in certain proportions, produce white, or pure
light. Each of these colours is a distinct principle,
having properties peculiar to itself, which properties
are not inherited by either of the other two. These
three colours, by blending together, make up the other
four, which complete what are commonly called the
seven colours of light. The other four, however, are
not simple, but compound; they are always found in
a decomposition of a ray of light, and sometimes
they are distinctly visible in the rainbow; but every one

The vegetable kingdom is but an included part of
the great and infinite kingdom of what is called inani-
mate or still nature. It consists of little organized
bodies, formed after the model of the great original
nature herself. These bodies not being self-existent,
as organized individuals, extract from the great source
of nature around them the nourishment necessary for
their support. They suck in also along with this
nourishment the poison which is inherent in their great
mother, which, when they are no longer able to re-
sist and overcome it, finally destroys them, and re-
duces them to their primitive elements, as part of the
great body of universal nature, the living mother of
us all, in whose womb we all live without being
born. There is no egress from nature; she gives sup-
port to all her offspring, but receives support from
none; for to her there is nothing external. These
vegetables are not possessed individually of will or
self-motion; they have no consciousness of separate
existence, but throughout the whole vegetable king-
dom the same family likeness of a Trinity is distinctly
to be traced. Independent of the grand three-fold
division of plants, which botanists call acotyledons,
monocotyledons, and decotyledons, we have three

The same grand threefold and sevenfold division
is observable in the animal world, more especially in
the human species. The solid or vital parts of the
body are three, the head where the intellect resides,
the trunk or breast, where the heart and lungs are
placed, and the abdomen or belly, where corruption
reigns, from which corruption, life, and health, and
intellect are extracted. The four extremities are not
vital, and, therefore, they form a distinct fourfold
department similar to that which we found in light and
vegetation. In the spinal bone or back bone, which
connects these vital parts of the body together, aud
constitutes their main support, containing the spinal mar-
row, the pith, and vigour of the whole animal system,
the same threefold division is observable. The cervical
vertebræ belong to the neck, and give support to
the head, they are seven in number.

The arterial or pure red blood is the only species of

and discharges as it passes through the system; and
the pulmonary blood, by working its way through
the air cells of the lungs, is filtered, purified, liqui-
fied, and prepared, for arterial circulation as at first.

hammer. Fire is never seen. The lucid glare of the
lightning is ignited air or gas; the flame of the candle
is ignited smoke, and the redness of the fire is merely
ignited coal. We may just as reasonably, therefore,
enumerate gravitation, or magnetism amongst the ele-
ments, as heat or caloric. They are all of them
merely an invisible bond of union, like the will in
man and other animals, which unites the three ele-
mentary principles of nature into one. To these three
elements the threefold division of the animal kingdom
corresponds, namely, land animals, birds, and fishes,
all these live in the omnipresent heat which per-
vades all nature, but there is no species of animal
which requires ignition or visible fire to support its
existence.

It may appear very ab-
surd and contradictory to maintain that heat and cold
are one and the same thing, or at least produced by
the same cause; but there is good reason to suppose
that this is the case. We know that in the electric fire
there are two distinct sexes, and that these two unite
and form a new compound, in all threefold. It has
been observed by the students of nature, that there
is a vast collection of electric matter at the poles.
The frequent appearance of the Aurora Boreales or
northern lights, give abundant evidence of this. The
co-existence of this electric matter with the intense
cold of the polar regions, gives a great degree of
plausibility to the supposition that cold may be pro-
duced by the mixture of the two electric substances
in a different proportion to that of heat. There are
many chemical changes more wonderful than this,
which modern discoveries have established as facts,
I will only mention one of them at present, which
bears a strong analogy with the supposition above al-
luded to. It is a curions property of light from which
it appears that two whites make a black. If you
take a board and bore two small holes in it, and place
a candle at each hole so as to let a ray of light pass
through each hole to a sheet of white paper opposite,
and contrive it in such a manner that the two rays
of light shall meet together at the same spot upon the
sheet of paper, then you will observe a bright spot
upon the surface of the paper where the rays meet.

family likeness throughout all the departments of nature,
that analogy may safely be received as demonstration
where facts cannot be procured.

world of fancy. To depart from either is to turn
our backs upon improvement, and to return to bar-
barism.

He wrote a work of natural
history." He spoke three thousand proverbs, and bis songs were a
thousand and five; and he spake of trees, from the cedar tree that
is in Lebanon, to the hyssop that springeth out of the wall. He
spuke also of beasts, and of fowl, and of creeping things, and of
fishes. And there came of all people to hear the wisdom of Solo-
mon, from all kings of the earth which had heard of his wisdom.”
This is a good example for us all, and a notable p.oof of the truth
of what I always maintain, that the study of nature is the true
sou ce of wisdom, and the true key of interpretation for the B.ble.
If the Christians refuse to follow the example of Solomon, they de-
part from the path of duty which the Bible itself points out to them.
Solomon is not only called a wise man, but it is said that none
would arise after him, that would equal him in wisdom. And, can-
sidering the age in which he lived, it a pears to me that this pre-form in this way than in any other.
diction has not yet been falsified. Solomon believed in God, studied
and admired his works, had no fear of the devil or hell-fire, re-
ceived all religions as equally true, and all proceeding from the same
source; eat his bread with joy, and drank his wine with a merry
heart, and took his chance of bereafter. So do I, with this difference,
that I cannot afford wine to drink, like the king of Israel, but that
does not grieve me much.
